data_in.description: Raw Hi-C FASTQ dataset from a public repository (GEO, SRA, or equivalent)

data_out.description: Hi-C map file in .hic format generated from raw FASTQ reads

domain_knowledge: The Juicer pipeline generates kilobase-resolution Hi-C contact maps from raw paired-end FASTQ reads through automated read alignment, deduplication, and contact matrix normalization.
Juicer 1.6 is the last stable release; Juicer 2 is under active development and available directly from the GitHub repository, with potentially different features or API.
The .hic output format is the standard artifact produced by Juicer and contains normalized contact frequency matrices suitable for downstream genomic feature annotation and visualization.
Pipeline configuration requires specification of reference genome, restriction enzyme recognition sites, and computational resource allocation (threads, memory) appropriate to dataset size.
Output validation requires confirmation that the .hic file is successfully generated with non-empty contact matrices and contains metadata required for downstream tools.

direct_checks: verify file_exists: .hic output file is generated in the pipeline working directory
verify file_format_is: output .hic file conforms to HiC file format specification (byte signature and internal structure)
verify script_runs: Juicer pipeline execution completes without fatal errors on test FASTQ dataset
verify row_count_equals or value_in_range: output .hic file size is non-zero and within expected range for Hi-C maps (robust to parameter choices in alignment and resolution settings)
verify output_matches_reference: generated .hic file can be loaded and parsed by standard Hi-C analysis tools (e.g., Juicer Tools), indicating structural validity
expert_review: Confirm that Hi-C contact matrix statistics (e.g., read pair counts, chromosome interaction distribution, cis/trans ratio) are consistent with expected values for the input dataset
Assess whether the generated .hic artifact exhibits expected biological signal (e.g., TAD structure, centromeric clustering) when visualized, versus artifactual patterns

expected_outputs: Hi-C map file in .hic format generated from raw FASTQ reads
finding: Juicer includes a pipeline that generates Hi-C maps from fastq raw data files as input, producing processed Hi-C map artifacts.
inputs: Raw Hi-C FASTQ dataset from a public repository (GEO, SRA, or equivalent)
methodology_summary: Select and clone Juicer version (1.6 stable or 2 development) from aidenlab/juicer GitHub repository.
Obtain raw Hi-C FASTQ dataset from a public data repository.
Configure Juicer pipeline parameters including reference genome, restriction enzyme, and computational resources.
Execute Juicer pipeline to process raw reads through alignment, contact matrix construction, and normalization.
Validation: verify that .hic output file is generated with non-empty contact matrices and contains required metadata.
missing_information: No changelog documenting version history, bug fixes, or changes between Juicer releases is available

task_description: Execute the Juicer pipeline end-to-end on a publicly deposited Hi-C FASTQ dataset to generate a .hic Hi-C map output file.

task_inputs.label: Raw Hi-C FASTQ dataset from public repository

task_objective: Execute the Juicer pipeline end-to-end on a public Hi-C FASTQ dataset and verify successful generation of a .hic output file.
title: Reconstruct the Juicer Hi-C Processing Pipeline from FASTQ to Hi-C Map
uncertainty_notes: This card was generated by the LLM-assisted pipeline and needs scientific expert review.
Each TracedClaim's evidence_span has been substring-checked against its source section; see logs/llm_calls.jsonl and capsules/<task_id>/quality_report.json for groundedness results.
Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: Juicer 1.6, Juicer 2.
workflow_description: 1. Clone the Juicer repository from aidenlab/juicer on GitHub and select either Juicer 1.6 (stable release) or Juicer 2 (development version) depending on deployment requirements. 2. Obtain a publicly deposited Hi-C FASTQ dataset (e.g., from GEO, SRA, or a similar public repository). 3. Configure the Juicer pipeline with appropriate parameters for the reference genome, restriction enzyme, and computational resources (number of threads, memory allocation). 4. Execute the Juicer pipeline on the raw FASTQ files to perform read alignment, contact matrix construction, and normalization. 5. Verify that the pipeline completes successfully and produces a .hic output file containing the processed Hi-C contact map.
